did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network with Asymmetric Kernels (AK-CNN) for feature extraction and a Support Vector Machine (SVM) for classification to classify dermatoscopic skin lesions as benign or malignant. The model was evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, achieving 97.33% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel 'AK-CNN+SVM' framework for skin cancer classification. It details specific methodology (preprocessing, feature extraction, classification), implementation details (HAM10000 dataset, MATLAB R2023b, specific hyperparameters), and reports new empirical results (97.33% accuracy) with comparisons to baseline models (ResNet-50+SVM) and ablation studies. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICIIP), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Cancer Classification Using Asymmetry Kernel Convolutional Neural Network and Support Vector Machine
journal: 2025 Eighth International Conference on Image Information Processing (ICIIP)
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel AK-CNN+SVM framework for classifying dermatoscopic skin lesions as benign or malignant to address challenges in early skin cancer diagnosis. The methodology involves preprocessing with image augmentation, feature extraction using an Asymmetric Kernel Convolutional Neural Network (AK-CNN), and classification via a Support Vector Machine (SVM). The model was evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, which was partitioned into training, validation, and test subsets. The proposed AK-CNN+SVM achieved an accuracy of 97.33%, outperforming the baseline ResNet-50+SVM model which achieved 93.00% accuracy. Ablation studies confirmed that the asymmetric kernels and SVM classifier significantly improved feature discrimination and overall performance. The results demonstrate the model's robustness and potential as an aid for clinicians in detecting skin cancer.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used as one of the two classes in the binary classification task. The study utilized a balanced subset of the HAM10000 dataset containing 500 malignant cases, specifically identified as melanoma in the context of the binary split (Benign vs Melanoma) mentioned in the conclusion and Table III.

Used as the other class in the binary classification task. The study utilized a balanced subset of the HAM10000 dataset containing 500 benign cases. Table III explicitly lists 'Benign Keratosis' as the column header for the benign class in the partition ratio, indicating the specific benign lesion type included in the subset.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (Benign vs. Malignant skin lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification of dermatoscopic skin lesions into 'benign' and 'malignant' classes using an AK-CNN+SVM framework. The model was evaluated on a balanced subset of the HAM10000 dataset containing 500 benign and 500 malignant cases. The primary metric reported is accuracy (97.33%), along with precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available standard dataset containing a large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions. The study utilized a balanced subset of this dataset.

use.training: Used for training the AK-CNN+SVM model (60% of the selected subset).
use.validation: Used to tune hyperparameters and monitor early stopping (10% of the selected subset).
use.testing: Used to evaluate performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) (30% of the selected subset).
use.other: The study notes that the full HAM10000 dataset contains 10,015 samples, but only a balanced subset of 1,000 samples (500 benign, 500 malignant) was randomly selected for this specific experiment.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Classification of skin lesions into benign or malignant classes by determining the optimal hyperplane to maximize the margin between classes.
To prevent overfitting during the training phase by monitoring validation loss/accuracy and stopping training when performance ceases to improve.
To improve the generalization capacity of the model by artificially increasing the diversity of the training dataset.
To analyze the contribution of specific components (asymmetric kernels, SVM classifier) to the overall model performance by comparing different configurations (CNN+SVM, AK-CNN+Softmax, AK-CNN+SVM).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and ablation studies without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.dataset_description: Balanced subset of 1,000 samples (500 Benign Keratosis, 500 Melanoma) from the HAM10000 dataset. Partitioned into Training (600), Validation (100), and Test (300) sets.

Comparison with Baseline (ResNet-50+SVM).notes: The proposed AK-CNN+SVM outperformed the ResNet-50+S baseline in all reported metrics.

Ablation Study.notes: Inclusion of asymmetric kernels improved feature discrimination capacity by 5.66% compared to CNN+SVM. Replacement of softmax classifier with SVM increased accuracy by 8.33% compared to AK-CNN+Softmax. The proposed model achieved a loss value below 0.3.

HAM10000.notes: The model was evaluated on a binary classification task (Benign vs. Melanoma). The confusion matrix indicated low misclassification rates for both classes.

architecture_used.main_model: AK-CNN+SVM (Asymmetric Kernel Convolutional Neural Network with Support Vector Machine)
architecture_used.input_size: 450 x 600 x 3 (original dataset dimension); resized during preprocessing

architecture_details.backbone: Custom CNN with asymmetric rectangular convolutional kernels
architecture_details.key_features: Use of asymmetric kernels (3x15, 3x11, 3x7, 3x5) to capture features in different orientations/aspect ratios inherent in dermoscopic images.

training_details.hardware: i7 processor, 16 GB RAM, 8 GB NVIDIA GeForce graphics card

classification_or_output_head.details: Two-class SVM used to classify features from the final fully-connected layer into benign or malignant. Utilizes kernel functions (Gaussian, polynomial, linear) to map data to higher-dimensional space for optimal hyperplane separation.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: AK-CNN+SVM (Asymmetric Kernel Convolutional Neural Network with Support Vector Machine)
preprocessing_techniques: Image enhancement using a Gaussian filter to preserve local structures (edges, textures, fine details) and remove noise
Image resizing to 224x224x3 dimensions to handle variety of sample dimensions in the database
Removal of air bubbles, artifacts, and noise from dermoscopic images
Data augmentation applied to the training subset only, including flipping, rotating (90 and 180 degrees), scaling, and shearing

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the training subset only; includes horizontal and vertical flipping.
Applied to the training subset only; specifically 90 and 180 degrees.
Applied to the training subset only to expand the training set and reduce overfitting.

conclusions: The proposed AK-CNN+SVM framework achieved an accuracy of 97.33% on a balanced subset of the HAM10000 dataset, outperforming the baseline ResNet-50+SVM model which achieved 93.00% accuracy.
The inclusion of asymmetric kernels in the CNN architecture improved feature discrimination capacity by 5.66% compared to a standard CNN with SVM.
Replacing the softmax classifier with a Support Vector Machine (SVM) increased the model's accuracy by 8.33% compared to the AK-CNN+Softmax configuration.
The model demonstrated strong generalization capabilities, evidenced by low deviation between training and validation accuracy curves and a loss value below 0.3.
The study utilized a balanced subset of 500 benign (Benign Keratosis) and 500 malignant (Melanoma) cases from the HAM10000 dataset to mitigate class imbalance issues common in skin lesion datasets.
The proposed method effectively classified both benign and malignant cases with low misclassification rates, highlighting its potential as an aid for early and accurate skin cancer diagnosis.
important_remarks: The study evaluates the model on a highly restricted, balanced subset of the HAM10000 dataset (1,000 samples: 500 Benign Keratosis and 500 Melanoma), rather than the full dataset or other external validation sets, limiting generalizability to other lesion types or class distributions.

The binary classification task (Benign vs. Melanoma) does not reflect the clinical reality of multi-class skin lesion diagnosis, as acknowledged by the authors who note future work is needed for multi-class evaluation.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the performance difference between the proposed AK-CNN+SVM (97.33%) and the baseline ResNet-50+SVM (93.00%).
The model was implemented and tested using MATLAB R2023b, which may limit reproducibility for researchers using standard deep learning frameworks like PyTorch or TensorFlow.

Data augmentation was applied only to the training subset, which is a correct practice, but the small size of the training set (600 samples) raises concerns about overfitting despite the use of early stopping and dropout.

The paper compares the proposed method against other techniques (Table VI) using metrics from different studies without re-evaluating them on the same specific 1,000-sample subset, making direct performance comparisons potentially invalid due to dataset distribution differences.

The preprocessing steps include removal of air bubbles and artifacts, but the specific algorithmic details or manual vs. automated nature of this step are not fully specified, posing a risk of data leakage if preprocessing parameters were tuned on the test set.
The hardware specifications (i7 processor, 16 GB RAM, 8 GB NVIDIA GeForce) are not detailed enough to assess computational efficiency or reproducibility of training times.
